data.name: DSA001AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A WebApi method is using Entity Framework DbContext to directly manipulate data through a LINQ query expression. WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics. Move the data-manipulation business logics into a more appropriate class, or even better, an injected service.

data.name: DSA001AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The WebApi method '{0}' is using Entity Framework DbContext to directly manipulate data through a LINQ query expression. WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics.

data.name: DSA001AnalyzerTitle
data.value: WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics through a LINQ query expression

data.name: DSA002AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A WebApi method is using Entity Framework DbSet to directly manipulate data through a LINQ fluent query. WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics. Move the data-manipulation business logics into a more appropriate class, or even better, an injected service.

data.name: DSA002AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The WebApi method '{0}' is invoking the method '{1}' of the DbSet '{2}' to directly manipulate data through a LINQ fluent query. WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics.

data.name: DSA002AnalyzerTitle
data.value: WebApi controllers should not contain data-manipulation business logics through a LINQ fluent query

data.name: DSA003AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Usually, business logics distinguish between "string with content", and "string NULL or without meaningfull content". Thus, statistically speaking, almost every call to `string.IsNullOrEmpty` could or should be replaced by a call to `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace`, because in the large majority of cases, a string composed by only spaces, tabs, and return chars is not considered valid because it doesn't have "meaningfull content". In most cases, `string.IsNullOrEmpty` is used by mistake, or has been written when `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` was not available. Don't use `string.IsNullOrEmpty`. Use `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` instead.

data.name: DSA003AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Don't use `string.IsNullOrEmpty`. Use `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` instead.

data.name: DSA003AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Don't use `string.IsNullOrEmpty`. Use `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` instead

data.name: DSA004AnalyzerDescription
data.value:
Using `DateTime.Now` into business logics potentially leads to many different problems: - Incoherence between nodes or processes running in different timezones (even in the same country, i.e. USA, Soviet Union, China, etc) - Unexpected behaviours in-between legal time changes - Code conversion problems and loss of timezone info when saving/loading data to/from a datastore
Security-wise, this is correlated to the CWE category “7PK” https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/361.html Cit: *"This category represents one of the phyla in the Seven Pernicious Kingdoms vulnerability classification. It includes weaknesses related to the improper management of time and state in an environment that supports simultaneous or near-simultaneous computation by multiple systems, processes, or threads. According to the authors of the Seven Pernicious Kingdoms, "Distributed computation is about time and state. That is, in order for more than one component to communicate, state must be shared, and all that takes time. Most programmers anthropomorphize their work. They think about one thread of control carrying out the entire program in the same way they would if they had to do the job themselves. Modern computers, however, switch between tasks very quickly, and in multi-core, multi-CPU, or distributed systems, two events may take place at exactly the same time. Defects rush to fill the gap between the programmer's model of how a program executes and what happens in reality. These defects are related to unexpected interactions between threads, processes, time, and information. These interactions happen through shared state: semaphores, variables, the file system, and, basically, anything that can store information."*

data.name: DSA004AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: In a i18n-compliant system, it's almost never a good idea to use System.DateTime.Now. Use System.DateTime.UtcNow instead.

data.name: DSA004AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Don't use `DateTime.Now`. Use `DateTime.UtcNow` instead

data.name: DSA005AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An execution flow must always be as deterministic as possible. This means that all decisions inside a scope or algorithm must be performed on a "stable" and immutable set of parameters/conditions. When dealing with dates and times, always ensure that the point-in-time reference is fixed, otherwise the algorithm would work on a "sliding window", leading to unpredictable results. This is particularly impacting in datasource-dependent flows, slow algorithms, and in-between legal time changes. Security-wise, this is correlated to the CWE category “7PK” (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/361.html) In order to avoid problems, set a `var now = DateTime.UtcNow` variable at the top of the method, or at the beginning of an execution flow/algorithm, and reuse that variable in all places instead of `DateTime.***Now`

data.name: DSA005AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Referencing multiple times `DateTime***.Now` or `DateTime***.UtcNow` in the same method, could lead to a non-deterministic point-in-time execution.

data.name: DSA005AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Potential non-deterministic point-in-time execution

data.name: DSA006AnalyzerDescription
data.value: General exceptions should never be thrown, because throwing them, prevents calling methods from discriminating between system-generated exceptions, and application-generated errors. This is a bad smell, and could lead to stability and security concerns. General exceptions that triggers this rule are: Exception, SystemException, ApplicationException, IndexOutOfRangeException, NullReferenceException, OutOfMemoryException and ExecutionEngineException prevents calling methods from handling true, system-generated exceptions differently than application-generated errors. See also MITRE, CWE-397 - Declaration of Throws for Generic Exception (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/397)

data.name: DSA006AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: General exceptions should never be thrown, because throwing them, prevents calling methods from discriminating between system-generated exceptions, and application-generated errors.

data.name: DSA006AnalyzerTitle
data.value: General exceptions should not be thrown by user code

data.name: DSA007AnalyzerDescription
data.value: When initializing a lazy field, use a robust locking pattern, i.e. the "if-lock-if" (aka "double checked locking") to efficiently ensure that the variable is not initialized multiple times, and that no race-conditions occurs.

data.name: DSA007AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: When initializing a lazy field, use a robust locking pattern, i.e. the "if-lock-if" (aka "double checked locking")

data.name: DSA007AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use the "if-lock-if" (aka "double checked locking") initialization pattern instead of a simple lock

data.name: DSA011AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Self-contained static singleton properties, particularly when they involve lazy initialization within the property itself, can lead to several problems, especially in multithreaded environments. Use a IoC/DI framework instead, or at least use proper locking when initializing the instance (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA011.md).

data.name: DSA011AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Avoid lazily initialized, self-contained, static singleton properties

data.name: DSA011AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Avoid lazily initialized, self-contained, static singleton properties

data.name: DSA008AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Is a common misunderstanding that the RequiredAttribute is somehow able to validate a not-nullable DateTime property. In reality, not-nullable types are ignored by the RequiredAttribute, so it doesn't make any sense to use it in this context. Remove the RequiredAttribute, or make the property nullable (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA008.md).

data.name: DSA008AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The RequiredAttribute has no impact on a not-nullable DateTime property. Remove it, or make the property nullable.

data.name: DSA008AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Do not use the RequiredAttribute for a non-nullable DateTime property

data.name: DSA009AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Is a common misunderstanding that the RequiredAttribute is somehow able to validate a not-nullable DateTimeOffset property. In reality, not-nullable types are ignored by the RequiredAttribute, so it doesn't make any sense to use it in this context. Remove the RequiredAttribute, or make the property nullable (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA009.md).

data.name: DSA009AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The RequiredAttribute has no impact on a not-nullable DateTimeOffset property. Remove it, or make the property nullable.

data.name: DSA009AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Do not use the RequiredAttribute for a non-nullable DateTimeOffset property

data.name: DSA012AnalyzerDescription
data.value: The "if not exists, then insert" pattern (also known as "check-then-act") is a non-atomic sequence that performs an existence check followed by an insert operation. This pattern is prone to TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) race conditions: between the moment the existence check completes and the insert executes, another thread or process could insert the same record, leading to duplicate entries and data corruption. This is particularly dangerous in database operations without a UNIQUE constraint. Use an atomic upsert operation (e.g., SQL MERGE, INSERT ... ON CONFLICT), add a UNIQUE constraint to the database, or adopt the EAFP (Easier to Ask for Forgiveness than Permission) approach by catching the duplicate key exception instead (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA012.md).

data.name: DSA012AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The "if not exists, then insert" pattern is a non-atomic check-then-act sequence prone to race conditions (TOCTOU). Use an atomic upsert, a UNIQUE constraint, or an EAFP approach instead.

data.name: DSA012AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Avoid the "if not exists, then insert" check-then-act antipattern

data.name: DSA017AnalyzerDescription
data.value: The "if not exists, then insert" pattern on a collection type that offers an atomic alternative is redundant and prone to TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) race conditions. The collection type provides a built-in operation that combines the check and insert atomically. Use the atomic operation instead of the check-then-act pattern (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA017.md).

data.name: DSA017AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The check-then-act pattern on '{0}' is not atomic and prone to race conditions. Use {1} instead.

data.name: DSA017AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use the collection's atomic operation instead of the check-then-act pattern

data.name: DSA018AnalyzerDescription
data.value: The "if not exists, then insert" pattern on a collection type without an atomic alternative is prone to TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) race conditions in multithreaded code. Between the existence check and the insert, another thread could modify the collection, leading to duplicates or corruption. Protect the check-then-act sequence with a lock or SemaphoreSlim, or consider switching to a collection type with built-in duplicate handling such as HashSet or ConcurrentDictionary (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA018.md).

data.name: DSA018AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The check-then-act pattern is not atomic and prone to race conditions. Protect with a lock or SemaphoreSlim, or switch to a collection type with built-in duplicate handling.

data.name: DSA018AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Protect the check-then-act pattern with a lock or use a collection with built-in duplicate handling

data.name: DSA013AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Minimal API endpoints mapped on a local IEndpointRouteBuilder (not a RouteGroupBuilder, and not received as a method parameter) should always have an explicit authorization configuration in their fluent chain. Without a call to RequireAuthorization() or AllowAnonymous(), the endpoint silently defaults to anonymous access, creating an unauthenticated attack surface. Every endpoint should make a conscious, reviewable authorization decision. See also: MITRE, CWE-862 - Missing Authorization (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/862.html) (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA013.md).

data.name: DSA013AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The Minimal API endpoint '{0}' does not have an explicit authorization configuration. Add .RequireAuthorization() or .AllowAnonymous() to make the authorization intent explicit.

data.name: DSA013AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Minimal API endpoints should have an explicit authorization configuration

data.name: DSA014AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Minimal API endpoints mapped on a RouteGroupBuilder should have an explicit authorization configuration, either directly on the endpoint or inherited from the route group. Without a call to RequireAuthorization() or AllowAnonymous() on the endpoint or on any ancestor group, the endpoint silently defaults to anonymous access, creating an unauthenticated attack surface. This analyzer traces authorization across method boundaries when the group is received as a parameter, verifying that all call sites pass a group with authorization configured. See also: MITRE, CWE-862 - Missing Authorization (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/862.html) (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA014.md).

data.name: DSA014AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The Minimal API endpoint '{0}' on a route group does not have an explicit authorization configuration. Add .RequireAuthorization() or .AllowAnonymous() to the endpoint or to its route group.

data.name: DSA014AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Minimal API endpoints on route groups should have an explicit authorization configuration

data.name: DSA015AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Minimal API endpoints mapped on an IEndpointRouteBuilder received as a method parameter should have an explicit authorization configuration. Since the builder is received from the caller, authorization may need to be configured at the call site. This analyzer traces authorization across method boundaries to verify that all call sites provide an authorized builder. If authorization cannot be confirmed at every call site, the endpoint is flagged. Add .RequireAuthorization() or .AllowAnonymous() directly to the endpoint, or ensure all callers provide an authorized builder. See also: MITRE, CWE-862 - Missing Authorization (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/862.html) (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA015.md).

data.name: DSA015AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The Minimal API endpoint '{0}' is mapped on a route builder received as a method parameter without explicit authorization. Ensure all call sites provide an authorized builder, or add .RequireAuthorization() or .AllowAnonymous() directly to the endpoint.

data.name: DSA015AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Minimal API endpoints on parameterized route builders should have an explicit authorization configuration

data.name: DSA016AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An enumeration method is invoked multiple times with identical arguments on the same receiver within the same scope. Each call re-enumerates the source, which is wasteful and potentially non-deterministic if the source is a deferred IEnumerable. Extract the result into a local variable and reuse it. This is particularly impactful when the source is backed by a database query, a network stream, or any non-materialized sequence where repeated enumeration may yield different results or cause redundant I/O (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA016.md).

data.name: DSA016AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The enumeration method '{0}' is invoked {1} times with identical arguments on the same receiver. Extract the result into a variable to avoid redundant enumeration and potential non-deterministic behavior.

data.name: DSA016AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Avoid repeated invocation of the same enumeration method with identical arguments

data.name: DSA019AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A deeply nested member access chain is repeated multiple times in the same scope. Repeated deep dereferencing reduces readability and may incur unnecessary overhead if the intermediate accesses involve computation, virtual dispatch, or property getters with side effects. Extract the common chain into a local variable and reuse it. The depth threshold is configurable via 'dotnet_diagnostic.DSA019.max_repeated_dereferenciation_depth' (default: 3). Fluent API prefixes can be excluded via 'dotnet_diagnostic.DSA019.excluded_prefixes' (comma-separated, e.g., 'Is.Not, Has.No') (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA019.md).

data.name: DSA019AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The member access chain '{0}' is repeated {1} times in the same scope. Extract it into a local variable.

data.name: DSA019AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Avoid repeated deeply nested member access chains

data.name: DSA020AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An async lambda that only awaits Task.FromResult is redundant. Task.FromResult already returns a completed Task, so the async modifier and await keyword create an unnecessary state machine. The C# compiler cannot perform this simplification automatically because removing async/await changes exception propagation semantics (exceptions in async lambdas are captured in the returned Task; without async, they propagate synchronously). Additionally, the compiler has no compile-time knowledge that Task.FromResult returns a completed task — it is just a regular method call. While the JIT optimizes synchronously completed awaits to avoid actual suspension, the state machine infrastructure (IAsyncStateMachine struct, AsyncTaskMethodBuilder, MoveNext dispatch) is still emitted and executed. Writing the code without the unnecessary async/await avoids this overhead at the source level, produces cleaner IL, and removes any dependency on runtime optimization quality across different .NET versions, JIT tiers, and platforms (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA020.md).

data.name: DSA020AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Redundant async/await on Task.FromResult. Remove the async modifier and the await keyword; the Task is already completed.

data.name: DSA020AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Remove redundant async/await on Task.FromResult

data.name: DSA021AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Entity Framework queries that do not include a TagWith() or TagWithCallSite() call lack the traceability markers required for rapid root cause analysis during security incidents and operational failures. When an anomalous, slow, or malicious query pattern is detected in production database logs, monitoring dashboards, or intrusion detection systems, the absence of a source-identifying tag forces incident responders to manually reverse-engineer the generated SQL back to application source code — a process that is error-prone, time-consuming, and potentially catastrophic under the time pressure of an active incident or attack. TagWithCallSite() automatically embeds the source file path, line number, and calling method name into the generated SQL as a comment, enabling immediate identification of the originating code path. TagWith() allows developers to add a custom descriptive label for contextual traceability. In the context of cybersecurity, this directly supports mitigation of CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption): when a resource-exhaustion attack targets the database layer, tagged queries enable rapid identification and surgical isolation of the offending code path without requiring a full application shutdown. In the context of IEC 62443 certification, query tagging is vital for the Traceability requirements under FR 2 (Use Control) and the Integrity verification requirements under FR 3 (System Integrity), enabling auditable, tamper-evident chains from observed database activity back to the originating application code and the identity context in which it executed (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA021.md).

data.name: DSA021AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The Entity Framework query materialized by '{0}' does not include a call to TagWith() or TagWithCallSite(). Untagged queries hinder root cause analysis and incident response traceability.

data.name: DSA021AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Entity Framework queries should be tagged with TagWith or TagWithCallSite for traceability

data.name: DSA022AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An arithmetic or bitwise expression inside a loop body references only variables that are not modified within that loop iteration, making it loop-invariant. The expression is redundantly recomputed on every iteration, wasting CPU cycles on identical results. While modern compilers perform Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) as a JIT optimization, this transformation is not guaranteed across all .NET runtimes, JIT tiers, and platforms; explicitly hoisting the computation to a variable declared before the loop ensures deterministic performance regardless of optimizer quality. In tight numerical loops, redundant multiplications and additions inside the innermost loop can degrade throughput by orders of magnitude, particularly when the loop operates over large data sets where the redundant operations compound into millions of wasted cycles. Additionally, hoisting invariant sub-expressions improves cache locality by reducing the instruction footprint of the hot loop body, allowing the CPU instruction cache to retain the performance-critical path more effectively. In the context of cybersecurity, this relates to CWE-405 (Asymmetric Resource Consumption / Amplification): an attacker who can influence the iteration count of a loop containing unnecessarily repeated computations can amplify the cost of each request, turning a linear-time operation into a practical denial-of-service vector. In the context of IEC 62443 certification, computational efficiency in control loops directly supports FR 7 (Resource Availability) by ensuring that processing resources are not wasted on redundant computations that could be avoided with straightforward code restructuring (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA022.md).

data.name: DSA022AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The expression '{0}' is loop-invariant and can be hoisted before the loop to avoid redundant computation on every iteration.

data.name: DSA022AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Hoist loop-invariant expression out of inner loop

data.name: DSA023AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Using string concatenation to build file or directory paths is error-prone and can lead to subtle bugs: missing or duplicated separators, platform-dependent behavior (backslash on Windows vs. forward slash on Unix), and potential path traversal vulnerabilities when user-controlled segments are not properly normalized. Path.Combine handles all of these concerns correctly by normalizing separators and joining path segments in a platform-independent manner. Security-wise, this is correlated to CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal') (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html). In the context of IEC 62443, proper path handling supports FR 3 (System Integrity) by ensuring that file system operations target the intended paths and are not susceptible to manipulation through separator injection or normalization inconsistencies (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA023.md).

data.name: DSA023AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Use 'Path.Combine' instead of string concatenation to build the file path argument for '{0}'. Manual path concatenation is error-prone and platform-dependent.

data.name: DSA023AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use Path.Combine instead of string concatenation to build file system paths

data.name: DSA024AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A string concatenation is used to compose a value passed to a parameter whose name indicates it represents a file system path or file name. Manual path concatenation is error-prone: missing or duplicated separators, platform-dependent behavior (backslash on Windows vs. forward slash on Unix), and potential path traversal vulnerabilities when user-controlled segments are not properly normalized. Path.Combine handles all of these concerns correctly by normalizing separators and joining path segments in a platform-independent manner. This rule complements DSA023 by covering methods outside the well-known System.IO types, detecting path-like parameters by name. The set of recognized parameter names is configurable via .editorconfig options dotnet_diagnostic.DSA024.exact_parameter_names and dotnet_diagnostic.DSA024.prefix_suffix_parameter_names. Security-wise, this is correlated to CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal') (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/22.html). In the context of IEC 62443, proper path handling supports FR 3 (System Integrity) by ensuring that file system operations target the intended paths and are not susceptible to manipulation through separator injection or normalization inconsistencies (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA024.md).

data.name: DSA024AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Use 'Path.Combine' instead of string concatenation to build the path-like argument for '{0}'. Manual path concatenation is error-prone and platform-dependent.

data.name: DSA024AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use Path.Combine instead of string concatenation for path-like parameters

data.name: DSA025AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An interpolated string is passed as the message template to an ILogger logging method. Interpolated strings are evaluated eagerly at the call site regardless of whether the log level is enabled, causing unnecessary string allocations and formatting overhead. Additionally, interpolated strings bypass structured log storage: the resulting message is a pre-formatted flat string that cannot be parsed back into discrete fields by log aggregation systems (Seq, Elasticsearch, Application Insights, Loki). This means log entries lose their queryability, correlation, and filtering capabilities. Use a constant message template with named placeholders and pass the values as separate arguments. This allows the logging framework to skip formatting entirely when the log level is disabled, and to store each parameter as a structured field for downstream analysis. This is consistent with the built-in CA2254 diagnostic, but provides an automated code fix to convert interpolated strings to structured templates. Security-wise, structured logging prevents accidental information leakage through uncontrolled string expansion and supports forensic log analysis during incident response (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA025.md).

data.name: DSA025AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Use a structured logging template instead of an interpolated string for the '{0}' call. Interpolated strings are evaluated eagerly and bypass structured log storage.

data.name: DSA025AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use structured logging template instead of interpolated string

data.name: DSA026AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A CancellationToken from an outer scope is used inside a lambda, delegate, or local function that has its own CancellationToken parameter. The inner parameter represents the cancellation scope that the caller intended for the inner operation. Using a captured outer token bypasses those semantics and can cause operations to ignore their intended cancellation signal — for example, a per-request timeout token is ignored in favor of an application-lifetime shutdown token, allowing the operation to run indefinitely and consume server resources. Use the nearest CancellationToken parameter instead. If both tokens are needed, link them explicitly via CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA026.md).

data.name: DSA026AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Use '{0}' (nearest CancellationToken) instead of '{1}' from an outer scope

data.name: DSA026AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use nearest scope CancellationToken

data.name: DSA027AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A string variable is concatenated using the '+=' operator or the 's = s + expr' pattern inside a loop (for, foreach, while, do-while). Each concatenation allocates a new string and copies all previous content, resulting in O(n^2) time complexity for n iterations. For loops with many iterations or large strings, this causes excessive memory allocations and GC pressure. Use a StringBuilder instead: call Append or AppendLine inside the loop and convert to string once after the loop completes. This eliminates the repeated allocations by writing into a resizable buffer. Security-wise, unbounded string growth inside a loop can be exploited for denial-of-service: an attacker who controls the iteration count or the concatenated content can force quadratic memory and CPU consumption, exhausting server resources (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA027.md).

data.name: DSA027AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: '{0}' is concatenated in a loop. Use StringBuilder for O(n) instead of O(n^2) performance.

data.name: DSA027AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use StringBuilder instead of string concatenation in a loop

data.name: DSA028AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A method (or property getter) whose return type is IEnumerable<T>, IReadOnlyCollection<T>, or IReadOnlyList<T> materializes the result with .ToList(), but the caller cannot use any List<T>-specific mutating members (Add, Remove, Sort, indexer assignment, etc.) through the declared return type. Using .ToArray() instead eliminates the unused internal capacity buffer (List<T> typically over-allocates by 2×) and the hidden _size bookkeeping field, producing a tighter allocation with better cache locality. From a security perspective, returning an array through an immutable interface makes it harder for callers to accidentally or maliciously mutate shared state via an unsafe downcast to List<T> — an array downcast to IReadOnlyList<T> is still mutable only through the concrete reference, which the caller does not hold (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA028.md).

data.name: DSA028AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Return type does not expose List<T> mutating members. Use .ToArray() instead of .ToList() to avoid unnecessary memory overhead.

data.name: DSA028AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Prefer ToArray over ToList when return type is a read-only interface

data.name: DSA029AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Is a common misunderstanding that the RequiredAttribute is somehow able to validate a not-nullable value type property. In reality, not-nullable value types (bool, int, long, decimal, float, double, byte, enums, structs, etc.) are ignored by the RequiredAttribute, so it doesn't make any sense to use it in this context. Remove the RequiredAttribute, or make the property nullable (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA029.md).

data.name: DSA029AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The RequiredAttribute has no impact on a not-nullable value type property. Remove it, or make the property nullable.

data.name: DSA029AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Do not use the RequiredAttribute for a non-nullable value type property

data.name: DSA030AnalyzerDescription
data.value: Entity Framework queries that materialize results without explicitly specifying a change tracking strategy (AsNoTracking, AsTracking, or AsNoTrackingWithIdentityResolution) rely on the DbContext default, which is usually full tracking. This can lead to unnecessary memory consumption and slower queries when the entities are only read, or to subtle bugs when the developer assumes tracking is disabled. Explicitly specifying the tracking strategy forces a conscious decision and makes the intent clear (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA030.md).

data.name: DSA030AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Entity Framework query materialized via '{0}' without an explicit change tracking strategy. Add .AsNoTracking() or .AsTracking() to make the intent explicit.

data.name: DSA030AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Entity Framework queries should explicitly specify a change tracking strategy

data.name: DSA031AnalyzerDescription
data.value: An Entity Framework query materializes entities but change tracking is provably unnecessary—either because the query projects to a non-entity type (anonymous type, tuple, primitive), or because the containing method does not modify or persist the materialized entities. Adding AsNoTracking() eliminates unnecessary snapshot allocations, reduces GC pressure, and improves materialization throughput. In security-sensitive contexts, it also closes the stale-state manipulation surface by detaching returned entities from the change tracker (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA031.md).

data.name: DSA031AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: Entity Framework query should use AsNoTracking(); '{0}' does not require change tracking

data.name: DSA031AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Use AsNoTracking for Entity Framework queries that do not require change tracking

data.name: DSA032AnalyzerDescription
data.value: A string literal appears multiple times in the same method body. Repeated string literals reduce maintainability: if the value needs to change, every occurrence must be found and updated, risking inconsistencies and subtle bugs. Extract the duplicated string into a constant (local or class-level) and reference the constant instead. The maximum allowed duplications and the minimum string length are configurable via 'dotnet_diagnostic.DSA032.max_duplications' (default: 2) and 'dotnet_diagnostic.DSA032.min_string_length' (default: 5) (https://github.com/DogmaSolutions/Analyzers/blob/main/docs/rules/DSA032.md).

data.name: DSA032AnalyzerMessageFormat
data.value: The string literal "{0}" appears {1} times in the same method body. Extract it into a constant.

data.name: DSA032AnalyzerTitle
data.value: Avoid duplicated string literals in the same method body
